title: 30 Tir — St. John Chrysostom & The Assembly
narrative:
The thirtieth and final day of Tir returns to Saint John Chrysostom, the golden-mouthed Archbishop of Constantinople, and his monthly commemoration closes the month of the Epiphany as fittingly as it closed the month of the Nativity. If in Tahsas John's doxology — 'Glory to God for all things' — was the seal of the mystery of the Incarnation, in Tir it is the seal of the mystery of the public manifestation. John Chrysostom preached on the Baptism of the Lord with extraordinary power; his sermons on Matthew's Gospel include meditations on the descent of the Spirit at the Jordan that remain among the most penetrating in patristic literature. He understood the Epiphany as the moment when the divine economy moves from concealment to disclosure: the same Truth that was hidden in the womb of the Virgin, hidden in the cave of Bethlehem, hidden in the years of the Holy Family's obscure life in Nazareth, is suddenly and publicly revealed at the Jordan as the Lord of all creation. John's golden mouth is the instrument through which that revelation continues to resound in the ears of the Church.

The feast of the Synaxis of the Three Holy Hierarchs — Basil the Great, Gregory the Theologian, and John Chrysostom — is celebrated on this final day of Tir, uniting in a single commemoration the three greatest theological voices of the Eastern Christian tradition. This feast, which the Byzantine calendar celebrates on the thirtieth of January, is placed here at the close of Tir with a perfection of theological symmetry: Basil opened the month on its first day, defending the divinity of the Spirit who descended at the Jordan; Gregory the Theologian appeared on the twenty-fifth day, illuminating the Trinitarian mystery of the Epiphany; and John Chrysostom, who has appeared on both the monthly commemoration days of this month, now gathers them both into the unity of the Synaxis. The three together constitute the supreme theological witness to what Timqat has proclaimed: the Holy Trinity manifested in the fullness of its divine life at the Jordan River.

Saint Adey Abeba, a local Ethiopian saint whose name in the Ge'ez carries the sense of 'flower of flowers' or 'the blooming one,' completes the assembly of this final day of Tir. The flower-saint who closes the month of the Epiphany brings to its conclusion a note of spring arising within the winter season: for the feast of Timqat has always been understood in the Ethiopian tradition not merely as the celebration of a past event but as the annual renewal of creation, the moment when the Spirit who moved over the primordial waters moves again over the blessed waters of the feast and causes the world to bloom again with the life of the new creation. Adey Abeba, the flower of flowers, is the image of what the Epiphany produces in those who receive it: the blossoming of the divine life within the human person, the spring of the Kingdom breaking through the winter of the fallen world.

The theological seal of this thirtieth and final day of Tir — and of the entire month of the Epiphany — is the doxology of the three great hierarchs gathered around the flower of the desert: 'Glory to God for all things.' Tir has moved from the cave of Bethlehem to the banks of the Jordan, from the whispered word of Gabriel to the thundering Voice of the Father, from the hidden mystery of the Incarnation to the public proclamation of the beloved Son. The waters have been hallowed, the heaven has been opened, the Spirit has descended, and the voice of divine love has broken the silence of the ages. The Church closes the mouth of the month with the golden mouth of Chrysostom and the blossoming of Adey Abeba, holding together the eloquence of the theologian and the simplicity of the flower — for it is in the unity of sublime theology and simple beauty that the Epiphany most fully expresses itself: the Most High God, manifested at the water, claiming all creation as His own beloved.
